policy_statement: Pilot electricity and heating demand response in City premises and develop a plan for adoption scale and schedule based on pilots.
mechanism_description: The City will pilot electricity and heating demand response in its premises and use the results to plan broader adoption, shifting loads to improve grid flexibility and reduce associated emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Load shifting/peak reduction → ↓ fossil peaker/marginal generation + ↑ renewable utilization → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Human activity/system changed: Electricity and heating demand profiles in buildings/facilities are actively managed (e.g., shifting HVAC, thermal storage, water heating, industrial/process loads) in response to price/grid signals or control algorithms.  
(2) Mediating physical process: Lower peak demand and more flexible load reduces dispatch of high-emissions marginal units, avoids inefficient part-load operation, and increases the system’s ability to absorb variable renewable generation (and/or optimize CHP/district heating operations).  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced combustion of fossil fuels in power/heat generation leading to lower CO₂ (and sometimes reduced upstream methane and local air pollutants), i.e., net GHG emissions reduction.
dominant_pathway_test: Shifting and reducing peak loads lowers reliance on carbon-intensive marginal generation (and improves renewable integration), thereby reducing GHG emissions from electricity/heat supply.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Demand response primarily affects emissions by changing load shapes so the grid uses less fossil marginal/peaker generation and can integrate more renewables.  
2) Why Mitigation over alternatives: While “flexibility” can improve reliability (an adaptation-like co-benefit) and can improve energy efficiency in some cases, the single biggest climate-relevant effect across policies using this mechanism is typically reduced GHG emissions from electricity/heat supply.  
3) Edge cases applied: If the grid is already near-zero carbon, or if shifted load moves consumption to higher-carbon periods, the mitigation benefit can be small or ambiguous; nevertheless the mechanism’s intended and most common dominant climate effect remains emissions reduction via cleaner dispatch.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy implementing demand response to increase flexibility is fundamentally aimed at altering dispatch and renewable utilization; this general mechanism most consistently maps to mitigation via reduced supply-side emissions.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Whether a given policy’s primary impact is peak shaving, renewable integration, capacity deferral, or cost savings depends on local grid mix/market design and should be assigned at the policy level with supporting evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Benefits depend on marginal emissions factors and program design; poorly designed shifting can increase emissions, and in very low-carbon grids the dominant effect may shift toward reliability/cost rather than GHG reduction.
key_indicators: “Demand response of electricity and heating will be piloted”; “in the City’s premises”; “Based on the pilots… will be estimated”; “a plan will be made for the extent and schedule of the adoption”
co_benefits: Potential cost savings on energy bills; improved grid reliability/peak management; possible local air quality improvements
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be interpreted partly as an infrastructure-investment (if pilots require BMS/controls upgrades), but the excerpt emphasizes piloting and developing an adoption plan rather than specifying capital procurement or incentives.
